nodes.description: Cutaneous syndactyly of the fourth and fifth fingers is the characteristic digital malformation in ODDD; some patients also have third-finger or foot syndactyly.

evidence.reference_title: [Neurological presentations of oculodentodigital dysplasia].
evidence.snippet: Typical features are syndactyly of IV-V or III-V fingers with/without feet syndactyly, anomalies of eyes, teeth, hair and nose.

nodes.description: In neurologically affected individuals, brain MRI can show white matter abnormalities consistent with cerebral hypomyelination.

evidence.reference_title: GJA1 Variants Cause Spastic Paraplegia Associated with Cerebral Hypomyelination.
evidence.snippet: Cerebral MR imaging revealed variable white matter abnormalities, consistent with a hypomyelination pattern

nodes.description: Permanent flexion contracture of the fingers occurs as part of the digital phenotype in some patients with ODDD.

evidence.reference_title: Oculo-dento-digital dysplasia: a systematic analysis of published dental literature.
evidence.snippet: Examination revealed unusual facial features, i.e., long narrow nose, hypertelorism, prominent epicanthal folds along with syndactyly and camptodactyly.

nodes.description: Impaired Cx43-mediated GJIC during embryonic development disrupts coordinated signaling in neural crest-derived craniofacial structures and developing limb buds. This leads to the characteristic narrow nose, digital syndactyly, and dental anomalies. Cx43 is essential for osteoblast and odontoblast differentiation and function.
nodes.id: Craniofacial, dental, and limb developmental anomalies

evidence.reference_title: Connexin 43 (GJA1) mutations cause the pleiotropic phenotype of oculodentodigital dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Expression patterns and phenotypic features of gja1 animal mutants, reported elsewhere, are compatible with the pleiotropic clinical presentation of oculodentodigital dysplasia.

nodes.description: Dental care should start early and combine meticulous oral hygiene, preventive care, treatment of caries and enamel defects, restorative rehabilitation, and multidisciplinary dental follow-up to preserve function and occlusion.

evidence.reference_title: Dental management of oculodentodigital dysplasia: a case report.
evidence.snippet: Early diagnosis with a proper treatment plan and meticulous oral hygiene program helps eliminate the necessity of multiple tooth extractions.

evidence.reference_title: Oculo-dento-digital dysplasia: a systematic analysis of published dental literature.
evidence.snippet: Immediate treatment should be focused on the correction of current oral condition and symptomatic treatment. In the long term, attention should be diverted to prevent tooth wear and maintaining the occlusal vertical dimension to establish adequate function.

nodes.description: GJA1 encodes connexin 43 (Cx43), the most widely expressed gap junction protein. In the autosomal dominant form, missense mutations produce mutant Cx43 that co-assembles with wild-type Cx43 (and other connexins) into dysfunctional gap junction channels, exerting a dominant-negative effect. This impairs intercellular communication in tissues that critically depend on Cx43: craniofacial structures, limb buds, eyes, teeth, brain, and skin. Mutations occur across all nine domains of the Cx43 protein and may cause misassembly of channels or alter channel conduction properties. The tissue- specific phenotypic expression reflects the differential dependence of each tissue on Cx43-mediated gap junction intercellular communication (GJIC) during development and postnatal life.
nodes.id: Dominant-negative GJA1 mutations disrupt gap junction communication

evidence.reference_title: Connexin 43 (GJA1) mutations cause the pleiotropic phenotype of oculodentodigital dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Gap junctions are assemblies of intercellular channels that regulate a variety of physiologic and developmental processes through the exchange of small ions and signaling molecules.

evidence.reference_title: Connexin 43 (GJA1) mutations cause the pleiotropic phenotype of oculodentodigital dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: These mutations may cause misassembly of channels or alter channel conduction properties.

evidence.reference_title: GJA1 mutations, variants, and connexin 43 dysfunction as it relates to the oculodentodigital dysplasia phenotype.
evidence.snippet: Mutations resulting in ODDD occur in each of the nine domains of the Cx43 protein, and we review our functional experiments and those in the literature, examining the effects of 13 different Cx43 mutations upon gap junction activity.

nodes.description: Defective enamel formation is a recurrent dental abnormality in ODDD and contributes to structural tooth fragility.

evidence.reference_title: Oculo-dento-digital dysplasia: a systematic analysis of published dental literature.
evidence.snippet: Enamel hypoplasia, hypomineralization, microdontia, pulp stones, curved roots, and taurodontism were common dental findings in ODDD.

evidence.reference_title: GJA1 mutations, variants, and connexin 43 dysfunction as it relates to the oculodentodigital dysplasia phenotype.
evidence.snippet: we present 28 new cases with 18 novel GJA1 mutations. We include tables summarizing the 62 known GJA1 nucleotide changes leading to Cx43 protein alterations and the phenotypic information available on 177 affected individuals from 54 genotyped families.

evidence.reference_title: Connexin 43 (GJA1) mutations cause the pleiotropic phenotype of oculodentodigital dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: We found mutations in the GJA1 gene in all 17 families with oculodentodigital dysplasia that we screened.

nodes.description: Glaucoma is a clinically important ocular complication in ODDD and may threaten vision.

evidence.reference_title: Oculodentodigital Dysplasia: A Case Report and Major Review of the Eye and Ocular Adnexa Features of 295 Reported Cases.
evidence.snippet: The most common eye features reported among all mutations were microcornea, microphthalmia, short palpebral fissures, and glaucoma.

evidence.reference_title: Heterozygous GJA1 variants with ocular phenotype: Missense in domain but truncation out of domain.
evidence.snippet: Of the 116 patients, glaucoma was observed in 26.7% (31/116), among whom 77.4% (24/31) of cases occurred in patients ≥10 years old.

nodes.description: Hand, foot, and limb evaluation should assess syndactyly, camptodactyly, and functional limitations, with referral to hand surgery or orthopedics when deformities impair function or require reconstruction.

evidence.reference_title: [Neurological presentations of oculodentodigital dysplasia].
evidence.snippet: Typical features are syndactyly of IV-V or III-V fingers with/without feet syndactyly, anomalies of eyes, teeth, hair and nose.

nodes.description: In autosomal recessive ODDD, homozygous mutations cause complete loss of Cx43 function rather than dominant-negative effects. The R33X truncation mutant fails to form functional channels and additionally exerts trans- dominant effects on co-expressed connexins (Cx43 and Cx40), potentially explaining the greater disease burden in some AR cases. The R76H mutant retains partial function with reduced conductance.
nodes.id: Loss-of-function mechanism in autosomal recessive ODDD

evidence.reference_title: Autosomal recessive GJA1 (Cx43) gene mutations cause oculodentodigital dysplasia by distinct mechanisms.
evidence.snippet: the trans-dominant effect of R33X together with its complete inability to form a functional channel may explain why patients harboring this autosomal recessive R33X mutant exhibit greater disease burden than patients harboring the R76H mutant.

evidence.reference_title: Autosomal recessive GJA1 (Cx43) gene mutations cause oculodentodigital dysplasia by distinct mechanisms.
evidence.snippet: the R76H mutant trafficked to the plasma membrane to form gap junction-like plaques, whereas the R33X mutant remained diffusely localized throughout the cell, including the nucleus.

nodes.description: Abnormally small corneas are a recurrent ocular finding in ODDD and often accompany microphthalmia.

evidence.reference_title: Oculodentodigital Dysplasia: A Case Report and Major Review of the Eye and Ocular Adnexa Features of 295 Reported Cases.
evidence.snippet: The most common eye features reported among all mutations were microcornea, microphthalmia, short palpebral fissures, and glaucoma.

nodes.description: Small teeth are a recurrent dental manifestation of ODDD.

evidence.reference_title: Oculo-dento-digital dysplasia: a systematic analysis of published dental literature.
evidence.snippet: Enamel hypoplasia, hypomineralization, microdontia, pulp stones, curved roots, and taurodontism were common dental findings in ODDD.

nodes.description: Small eyes are among the most commonly reported ocular manifestations of ODDD.

evidence.reference_title: Oculodentodigital Dysplasia: A Case Report and Major Review of the Eye and Ocular Adnexa Features of 295 Reported Cases.
evidence.snippet: The most common eye features reported among all mutations were microcornea, microphthalmia, short palpebral fissures, and glaucoma.

nodes.description: Neurologic follow-up is indicated when spasticity, ataxia, dysarthria, seizures, neurogenic bladder, or other neurologic signs are present. Care includes neurologic examination, brain MRI when indicated, seizure treatment, spasticity and mobility management, and bladder/bowel management.

evidence.reference_title: [Neurological presentations of oculodentodigital dysplasia].
evidence.snippet: In about 30% of patients neurological disorders appear later in life: progressive spastic paraparesis, neurogenic bladder/bowel, ataxia, white matter lesions on MRI.

evidence.reference_title: GJA1 Variants Cause Spastic Paraplegia Associated with Cerebral Hypomyelination.
evidence.snippet: Patients with the more prominent basal ganglia abnormalities were the most disabled ones.

nodes.description: Cx43 is the predominant gap junction protein in astrocytes and plays a critical role in CNS homeostasis. Disrupted astrocytic gap junction networks impair potassium buffering, metabolite exchange, and coordinated neural activity, contributing to spastic paraplegia, seizures, ataxia, and white matter abnormalities observed in ODDD.
nodes.id: Neurological dysfunction from impaired CNS gap junctions

evidence.reference_title: GJA1 mutations, variants, and connexin 43 dysfunction as it relates to the oculodentodigital dysplasia phenotype.
evidence.snippet: Neurological problems, including dysarthria, neurogenic bladder disturbances, spastic paraparesis, ataxia, anterior tibial muscle weakness, and seizures, are known to occur

nodes.description: GJA1/Cx43 disruption contributes to ocular developmental anomalies such as microphthalmia and microcornea, while ciliary body cysts and progressive angle closure can drive glaucoma in some patients. This makes ocular disease both a developmental feature and a surveillance priority.

evidence.reference_title: Oculodentodigital Dysplasia: A Case Report and Major Review of the Eye and Ocular Adnexa Features of 295 Reported Cases.
evidence.snippet: Mutations most commonly affect the extracellular-1 and cytoplasmic-1 domains of connexin-43 (gene product of GJA1), predominately manifesting in microphthalmia and microcornea.

evidence.reference_title: Case report: imaging and treatment of ophthalmic manifestations in oculodentodigital dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: This is one of the first reported cases of severe chronic angle- closure glaucoma secondary to ciliary body cysts in a patient with oculodentodigital dysplasia.

nodes.description: Patients need regular ophthalmologic surveillance for microcornea, microphthalmia, angle narrowing, ciliary body cysts, and glaucoma. Management includes tonometry and gonioscopy, ultrasound biomicroscopy when indicated, topical pressure-lowering therapy when useful, and glaucoma surgery such as tube shunt placement for refractory angle-closure disease.

evidence.reference_title: Case report: imaging and treatment of ophthalmic manifestations in oculodentodigital dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Subsequent serial gonioscopy every 3 months showed gradual narrowing of the right eye angle and finally three-and-a-half years after tube placement of the left eye, her right eye IOP became uncontrolled with medications alone and a tube shunt was similarly placed in the right eye.

evidence.reference_title: Case report: imaging and treatment of ophthalmic manifestations in oculodentodigital dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: We believe that early screening for ciliary body cysts is important in patients with oculodentodigital dysplasia.

nodes.description: Calcified pulp stones are reported among the recurrent radiographic dental findings in ODDD.

evidence.reference_title: Oculo-dento-digital dysplasia: a systematic analysis of published dental literature.
evidence.snippet: Enamel hypoplasia, hypomineralization, microdontia, pulp stones, curved roots, and taurodontism were common dental findings in ODDD.

nodes.description: Seizures are part of the broader neurologic phenotype reported in ODDD.

evidence.reference_title: GJA1 mutations, variants, and connexin 43 dysfunction as it relates to the oculodentodigital dysplasia phenotype.
evidence.snippet: Neurological problems, including dysarthria, neurogenic bladder disturbances, spastic paraparesis, ataxia, anterior tibial muscle weakness, and seizures, are known to occur

nodes.description: Progressive lower-extremity spasticity is a prominent neurologic manifestation of ODDD and may emerge later in life.

evidence.reference_title: [Neurological presentations of oculodentodigital dysplasia].
evidence.snippet: In about 30% of patients neurological disorders appear later in life: progressive spastic paraparesis, neurogenic bladder/bowel, ataxia, white matter lesions on MRI.
